name: Taylor's Series
tagline: Approximate or evaluate a function by its polynomial expansion around a point. Three master expansions (e^x, sin x, cos x) plus the remainder term cover most quant interview applications.

blocks.text: Taylor's series expands f(x) around x_0 as f(x) = f(x_0) + f'(x_0)(x-x_0) + f''(x_0)/2! * (x-x_0)^2 + .... When x_0 = 0 it is called the Maclaurin series. Three expansions show up so often that they should be memorized: e^x = sum of x^n/n!, sin(x) = x - x^3/3! + x^5/5! - ..., and cos(x) = 1 - x^2/2! + x^4/4! - ....

Beyond approximation, Taylor's series proves identities (Euler's formula e^(i*theta) = cos(theta) + i*sin(theta)) and inequalities (Bernoulli: (1+x)^n > 1 + nx) by examining the remainder term. The remainder R_n(x) = f^(n+1)(x_bar) / (n+1)! * (x-x_0)^(n+1) for some x_bar between x_0 and x carries the truncation error and often has a definite sign.

blocks.label: Mental model
blocks.text: Taylor's series turns a hard nonlinear function into a polynomial. If you only need 'close to x_0', use a few terms. If you need an exact identity (e^(i*theta)), use the full series and pattern-match. If you need an inequality, examine the sign of the remainder.

whenToUse: Approximating f(x) for x near x_0 (linear, quadratic, etc).
Proving identities involving e, sin, cos, and complex numbers (via Euler's formula).
Proving inequalities by showing the remainder term has a definite sign.
Deriving Newton's method (linear Taylor) and its quadratic-convergence rate (next-order remainder).
Anywhere f is hard but its derivatives at one point are easy.
recipe: Choose the expansion point x_0 -- usually 0 or a value where f and its derivatives are simple.
Compute f(x_0), f'(x_0), f''(x_0), ... up to the order you need.
Plug into the Taylor formula. For Maclaurin (x_0 = 0): f(x) = sum of f^(n)(0)/n! * x^n.
If proving an identity: pattern-match against known series.
If proving an inequality: write f(x) = T_n(x) + R_n(x) and show R_n has the right sign.
commonPitfalls: Forgetting the n! in the denominator -- a famous source of factor-of-n errors.
Truncating too early when the next term changes the answer significantly.
Using the series outside its radius of convergence (e.g., the Maclaurin series of 1/(1-x) only converges for |x| < 1).
Dropping the remainder term when its sign matters for the conclusion.
